speaker: interviewer
text: --third tape with Mr. David Mandel. Mr. Mandel, you were telling us about the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. While in the concentration camp in Jaworzno, we had selection every once in a while. All those who were incarcerated-- most of them-- most of them were reduced to nothing but skin and bones within three months. And they were-- we had to go through, run across, a certain area naked. And they looked us over, and anyone not deemed worthy of make-- keeping alive were selected. And they were put on a truck, and they were carted off to-- to-- to Birkenau. And they were all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] destroyed, killed, murdered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the crematorium. Our rations consisted of-- we had to get up about 4 o'clock in the morning, and we had to stand in line. And they counted us. They called that 'appel.' And they gave us some kind of a hot substance, a tea-like-- a grassy-type of water. And that's all we had for breakfast. And after that, we had to line up again as we were heading for work. And as we left the gate, they counted us. When we arrived to the workplace, they counted us. As they broke us down into the details, they counted us. At noon, we had to assemble again in the middle of the-- of the workplace. They counted us. And we had to line up, and they dished out a-- a bowl of soup. I remember we were lucky if we got the bottom of the barrel, that was thicker. If we were unlucky, we got the watery part of it that was on the top. And anybody who moved out of line and in and out got a terrible, cruel beating. We didn't dare move out. At night, when we came back, we had to-- after work, they counted us. When we left the place of work, they counted us. As we entered the camp again, they counted us. We had to line up in front of every barrack. They counted us. And after that, they gave us our bread, which was a piece of black bread. Sometimes it had a slice of margarine with it. Sometimes a slice of liverwurst. And that was our meager rations. It was a starving rations. I don't know how many calories, but it just wasn't enough to-- to survive on. I was one of the youngest ones in the camp. Eventually, I moved from this power plant. I worked in a coal mine. I was switching tracks. I was a kid. There was a civil Polish engineer who would give me a piece of bread once in awhile. I would help out in the kitchen. Now that I look back, maybe fellow inmates saw to it that I'd get a job, that I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] survive. We didn't think that we could-- anybody would ever survive these unsurvivable conditions. But I was given a chance. And I was a little bit better off than most. And they wanted me, perhaps, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be a eyewitness to what took place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- I-- I witnessed father and son fighting over a piece of bread. Son stealing from the father. I witnessed what went on. I was sort of glad that I wasn't with my father and my brother. It reminds me of a drowning man-- somebody trying to save him, and they both drown. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- I had a bad tooth. I remember I went to the Krankenbau. Two guys held me down, and somebody yanked out, without any Novocaine. They pulled out my tooth. I volunteered on Sundays. Every other Sunday we had off. I volunteered-- to get out of the camp-- to clean the SS barracks. It was very dangerous to volunteer for anything. You never knew what was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was awaiting you. Anyone who tried to escape-- there was a escape attempt one time at Jaworzno. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the group, they tried to dig a tunnel under the fence, and they were caught. We came back. Everyone in the group was hung to teach us a lesson-- that's what will happen to you. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And-- and then one day, in 1945, I think-- it was in January, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a cold night. It was snowing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They woke-- they awakened us. We had to line up and put our-- our clothes on. Our clothes consisted of-- of a-- of a shirt made out of some kind of a cotton fabric, with no collar; a pajama cotton jacket with a cotton pajama pants; a piece of cloth for socks; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoes that were canvas top with wooden soles. The soles must've been an inch thick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had caps, cotton caps. We had a cotton coat, like a robe. We had to line up in the cold of the winter, and they were evacuating the camp. I guess the Russians started an invasion, and the Nazis didn't want to leave anybody behind. They were evacuating all camps. They were burning the barracks. But all those sick who couldn't march, they didn't want any witnesses to tell the world of these atrocities that took place. At the time, we were about maybe 3,000 to 5,000 of us. We marched. It was difficult to go on the road with these wooden shoes. The snow clung to it. It's just like walking on skates. Anybody who twisted an ankle and couldn't continue was shot. Anybody who tried to escape was shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After about three days, we-- we-- three nights, we walked and slept in fives. The three in the middle slept, and two on the-- on the outside were walking. And you can sleep and walk. It's amazing how much a human being can endure. I remember one time we stopped in a-- on a farm, in a big barn. Everybody piled in, and we piled up on top of one another. All those who were on the bottom probably suffocated. People were taking off clothes from the dead, from one another, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to change shoes. Anybody who lost a cap, you know, grabbed somebody else's cap. People were fending for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We became worse [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] than animals. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] We finally arrived in a camp called Blechhammer, and the guards left us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the gate was open. Some of us wandered out into the woods. One time, I remember we found out where the warehouse was, and we just went as a group and tried to break the door open. And we went in there, and we just brought all the foodstuff out. And people were just fighting, scratching. And I remember suddenly there was a machine gun erected opposite. And they started machine-gunning us. This friend of mine and I-- who I had befriended, another French boy-- we were buddy-buddy. We were on the side, and somebody came through with some bread. We wrestled him to the ground. We took a bread away from him. And I remember-- and then the guards left. We found the-- a cellar with potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember the barracks were burning. There were bodies strewn all over. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It didn't bother me at all. Now that I see blood, I faint. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was frying potatoes, sitting on dead bodies. There were people begging for help. People were fending for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am asked how I could survive. They say survivors have a guilty conscience. I didn't help. I looked out for myself. What went through my mind is that I have to stay alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have three younger brothers. I have a sister. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They'll need me. And that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kept me going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were waiting-- we knew already-- word got out, you know, that the Russians are coming. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but, lo and behold, one night we were awakened with gunshots. We had to stand, line up again, then march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blechhammer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to stand in line. They went through with bayonets into the sleeping quarters. They set the barracks on fire. They were shooting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stand in-- we stood in line. I remember we left the camp. We were on the-- on the road again. Cold, snow. It's amazing-- it's amazing how I never caught a cold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were-- came to a-- through a village, I remember, about maybe two, three hours after we left the camp, middle of the night. We turned the corner. The guard hasn't turned the corner yet. I had a blanket with me, a rolled up blanket. I threw it. My friend and I, three others, ran off the road into this yard. There was a fence, snow knee-deep, jumped over the fence. These three after us, these three guys, these three inmates. By that time, the guard turned the corner. He hollered, halt. Stop. But there was no stopping. We ran. They opened up fire. Those three never made it. I remember this other kid who was about 18-- I was 15-- crawled in the snow. We zigged and zagged. The bullets were flying. There was woods-- a forest nearby. We had to go across a little creek that was frozen. My-- my friend got hit. A bullet grazed his ankle. We were hiding out in these woods, a 15-year-old, an 18-year-old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Didn't know which way to go. [SIGHS] There were some skeletons, you know, like, achtung. Mines, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- in the woods we found a-- a cottage. It was like a-- it must've been a resort, because there was something like a motel. It had rooms. And it had, I remember, it had a bomb shelter. And we were looking for footprints. We were sniffing for humans. We were like animals. We threw pebbles at these shutters to see whether anybody is around. And we finally convinced nobody was around. We went in there. We found pieces of frozen bread, some sack of sugar, some frozen potatoes. It was nourishment. We were there. We-- I remember we took a-- a-- [SIGHS] a-- mattresses from these rooms, and we piled them into one room. And we sort of created a hole in between the mattresses. That's where we slept. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at night, we would open up the shutters, then we would get some snow and melt it for water. And then one day, our shutter was opened a little bit, and we noticed a couple civilians approaching this cottage. And-- and they yanked the thing open. My friend jumped aside, and I was frozen. I couldn't move. These two civilians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had that scared look on their face like they saw a living skeleton. And I was scared. And-- and they told us that the Russians have been in for a couple days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to the road. I remember we had to go up an embankment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Russians were marching towards Germany. And there was a Russian Jewish soldier who spoke out to us in Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he took us to a-- this German family, a husband and wife, an elder couple. And he made them promise to feed us and take care of us. And he says, if not, he's going to come back. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, this German couple cooked for us-- slowly. And we ate. I swear I could see myself getting heavier, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was important that we eat slowly and the right food, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I read where these liberate-- these survivors who were liberated by the Americans, they were given these rich food and their systems couldn't take it, and it was like poison. After living through hell, they died because their stomach couldn't take it. We went back towards Poland. I remember in Katowice, there was a Red Cross. That's the first time I encountered the Americans. The women had lipstick on and a lot of makeup. They gave me shots. My face was broken out. And I don't know what kind of shots I got. And-- [SIGHS] and then we stayed there for a few days. We went into the city, and the city was deserted. There were bodies in the gutters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gliwice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what day and year-- month and year was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- in-- in-- that was in-- in January-- the end of January, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we were-- this happened just a few days, you know. On January 17, 1944, the Nazis emptied the camp, and we were on this march. And we were in Blechhammer. So it was about 14 days, somewhere around there, you know, that I went through all of that. And then I was heading back. I was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians. I didn't find out-- you know, I found out after the war. In 1982, when we had this-- I'm going to backtrack back.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1982, when we had the first gathering of survivors in Washington, DC, that's when survivors started opening up to talk about their experiences. Before that, you know, very few spoke, because it was just an unbelievable story. It was very hard to believe. But as a group, I think we gathered strength, you know? And it became all of a sudden believable.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we-- when I was in Washington, DC, in 1982, they were trying to make matches where they-- everyone went through what-- via-- via computer, and they found a match-- someone who-- a survived from the same camp that I was, Jaworzno. When I talked to him, he told me that he went along with this march. Eventually they put them on open carts, and they-- most of them died. It was cold. They didn't feed them. And they ended up in Buchenwald. In-- in-- in April-- they were still with the Nazis for February, March, and April. For another two and a half months. And I was free already. And very few-- only 65 survived. Only 65. And the camp-- I have-- I have documents via a museum. You know, Jaworzno had over 13,000-- 13,000 inmates off and on. And so far, I've met only three survivors. Three survivors. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, while I was in-- after-- in Katowice, you know, I was given, by the Red Cross, shots. I went into this town, Gliwice, and-- and there were stores. I went to a laundromat, and I got myself outfitted.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left. I threw my striped clothing away, and I put on, I remember, a corduroy riding pants. And I found a pair of boots. And I had shirts. And I had a jacket. And I had a cap. And I had a suitcase full of linen. And I went into a-- a dentist office. I had a-- I had a toothache. And I took all kinds of tools, and I put it-- I put it in the suitcase. And-- and my-- my friend and I were separated. He went with the French group, and they put me with the Czechoslovakian group. And I was sent to Poland, Krakow, Poland. There was a center there. And so there I was for a few days. And I ran into a fellow from my home town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who put you in that group with the Czechoslovakians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Red Cross, they divided everybody up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. They divided.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, probably 1st of February.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: End of-- 1st of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or thereabouts. You know, I don't know. [SIGHS] Anyway, I was in Krakow, the center where survivors were gathered and being taken care of. It was a center, like. And they fed us. And so I ran into this fellow from my hometown. And he recognized me. And he told me that he saw my dad alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked him, where? And he told me, so I ran to the station, to the main station, in Krakow. And, as I was heading that way, I saw these refugees coming off the train with their garb clothing. And they were heading, you know, with some guides to this center. And so I kept asking anybody, have you seen a man-- man by the name of Isaac Mandel from Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody spoke up, yes. He knows him. And he was on the train. I couldn't find him. He says, the train stopped on the outskirts, and some got off. So I ran all the way to the outskirts of Krakow, and I couldn't find anybody. So there was no point for me to go back, so I came back. And I ran into this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Went back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went back to Krakow to this Red Cross center. And I ran into this friend of mine, this fellow from Munkacs. And he took me by the hand. He says, come with me. And I went to this room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was my dad. [SOBBING] He-- he survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And how he survived, is a-- is a miracle, because he's a sugar diabetic-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he needed-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] he needed insulin. And so I went. I had all that stuff. I went to the black market, and I saw-- sold all those tools and all those sheets, and so on. And I traded for insulin. I came back, you know, within insulin. He was able to eat, and he started gaining strength. He weighed nothing but 60, 70 pounds.
